perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised regarding securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: The original text acknowledges awareness of insider trading prohibitions. The modified text weakens this acknowledgment, omitting the specific prohibition against using non-public information for trading or tipping, creating potential legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By removing the specific reference to the prohibition on using non-public information, the agreement weakens the warning against insider trading, potentially exposing the Receiving Party to legal liability under Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 if they engage in trading based on the Confidential Information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the specific prohibition against using non-public information for trading securities, which the original text explicitly acknowledged. This omission weakens the warning against insider trading. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By removing the specific reference to the prohibition on using non-public information, the modified agreement creates a loophole where the Receiving Party might argue they were not explicitly informed about the illegality of trading on non-public information, potentially reducing their legal liability and undermining the purpose of Rule 10b-5. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition because a legally relevant piece of information was removed.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines that a party has breached this Agreement, then such party shall be liable and pay to the non-breaching party the legal fees and expenses such non-breaching party has incurred in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own legal fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides for fee-shifting in the event of a breach. The modified text removes this provision, stipulating that each party bears its own costs. This alteration could contradict New York law which, under certain circumstances, implies a right to attorney's fees for prevailing parties in contract disputes, especially when one party's conduct is particularly egregious or in bad faith. By explicitly denying fee shifting, the contract limits remedies potentially available under New York law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 8101
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 8101 grants discretion to the court to award costs in an action, which can include attorney's fees under certain circumstances (though not automatically). By stating each party bears their own fees, this agreement attempts to override the court's discretionary power to award fees, and any common law exceptions related to attorney's fees, potentially contradicting the spirit and application of this procedural rule.

Costs in an action. The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.

perturbation.justification: The modified text, by stating each party bears its own legal fees, directly contradicts the principle outlined in NY CPLR § 8101. This section states that 'The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.' The contract attempts to override the court's ability to award costs to the prevailing party, potentially undermining the statute's intent, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof. Notwithstanding the foregoing, (a) the Receiving Party’s legal department and/or outside counsel may keep one copy of the Confidential Information (in electronic or paper form) and, with respect to the Receiving Party’s Representatives who are accounting firms, such firms may keep one copy of the Confidential Information, in each case, if required to comply with applicable law or regulation and (b) the Receiving Party and its Representatives may retain Confidential Information to the extent it is “backed-up” on its or their (as the case may be) electronic information management and communications systems or servers, is not available to an end user and cannot be expunged without considerable effort; provided, that any such information so retained pursuant to clauses (a) and (b) of this Section 7 shall be held in compliance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of eighteen months from the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Promptly upon the written request of the Company, the Receiving Party will return all copies of the Confidential Information to the Company, and all notes, studies, reports, memoranda and other documents or materials prepared by the Receiving Party or its Representatives that contain or reflect any Confidential Information shall be destroyed. Notwithstanding the return to the Company or destruction of Confidential Information pursuant to this Section 7, the Receiving Party and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their 4 confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement for the term hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows the Receiving Party's legal and accounting representatives to retain a copy of confidential information to comply with the law and allows for backed up copies. The modified text removes this exception, mandating complete destruction of all copies. This could create a conflict with professional regulations in New York that require attorneys and accountants to maintain records of their work for a certain period, thereby introducing legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Rules of Professional Conduct, Rule 1.15(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 1.15(c) of the New York Rules of Professional Conduct requires attorneys to maintain complete records of client funds and property coming into their possession and to preserve those records for a period of seven years after termination of the representation. While this agreement does not explicitly address client funds, the deletion of the exception allowing retention for legal compliance could be interpreted as conflicting with this rule if the 'Confidential Information' is construed as client property or related to a legal matter where record keeping is necessary.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: legal ethics | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you legal ethics Overview Legal ethics broadly refer to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system given the important role and influence they have in society. Because of their role and their close involvement in the administration of law, lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. Most commonly, legal ethics refers to these rules of professional responsibility : the actual responsibilities lawyers must follow by law such as client confidentiality. However, legal ethics can also refer to the discussion on broader moral principles that societies place on lawyers that may not be legally required. Professional Responsibility The law governing the professional responsibility of lawyers, since they are admitted to practice by states, is largely governed by the law of each state. However, all states follow more or less the Model Rules of Professional Conduct created by the ABA , and federal courts and agencies set their own practice rules and apply specific rules like those in the Sarbanes-Oxley Act . While the laws of each state actually govern, the Model Rules and their comments greatly influence the decisions of courts applying professional responsibility laws. The Model Rules set mandatory and discretionary rules and principles on a wide variety of lawyer practice including: client confidentiality , conflicts of interest , fiduciary responsibilities , commingling , and general malpractice . Should a lawyer breach these rules, state courts and enforcement agencies have wide discretion to punish the lawyer and breaching some of the rules like conflicts of interest can impact the outcome of the lawyer’s matters. Broader Legal Ethics Legal ethics can refer to a higher set of norms that lawyers should follow that may not be required by set legal rules. Many of the model rules try to include some of these broader principles. However, the model legal rules tend to be considered a floor of expected requirements of lawyers. Legal ethics can refer to the broader principles lawyers should strive for to ensure the protection and adequate representation of clients. While codes try their best to encourage ethical conduct of lawyers, the legal profession for centuries has struggled with balancing client protections with the often conflicting interests of lawyers and other clients. Thus, legal ethics often involves discussion of the ideals of lawyer conduct, irrespective of the mandates of legal rules. Further Resources For more information on the legal ethics rules and debates, see the Model Rules for Professional Conduct , this ABA Guide to State Ethics Resources , this Duke Law Guide to Legal Ethics Requirements , and this Notre Dame Journal of Law, Ethics, & Public Policy Article on the differences between and limitations of legal codes and ethical principles. [Last reviewed in March of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal education and writing legal history THE LEGAL PROCESS legal practice/ethics wex articles legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the exception allowing the Receiving Party's legal and accounting representatives to retain a copy of confidential information to comply with the law, and removes the allowances for backed up data. The Perturbation Explanation argues this conflicts with professional regulations in New York that require attorneys and accountants to maintain records of their work for a certain period, thereby introducing legal risk. The Law Explanation cites Rule 1.15(c) of the New York Rules of Professional Conduct, which requires attorneys to maintain complete records of client funds and property for seven years, suggesting a conflict if 'Confidential Information' is construed as client property or related to a legal matter where record keeping is necessary. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that legal ethics broadly refers to the unique responsibilities of lawyers and the legal system and that lawyers are subject to special standards, regulation, and liability. Removing the exception in the contract could potentially expose attorneys to liability if they are required to retain records under professional conduct rules, hence the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Without the Company’s prior written consent, which may be withheld by the Company in its sole discretion, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its Representatives not to) initiate, other than through the Company’s financial and legal advisors or such other persons, as designated by the Company in writing, any (a) communication concerning the Confidential Information, (b) requests for meetings with management of the Company in connection with the possible Transaction or other transaction between the parties or (c) communication relating to the business of the Company or the possible Transaction, in each case, with any officer, director or employee of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Without the Company’s prior written consent, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its Representatives not to) initiate any communication relating to the business of the Company with any officer, director or employee of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: The original text permits communication through designated advisors. The modified text removes the exception for communicating through the Company’s financial and legal advisors, broadly prohibiting any communication about the business. This could be interpreted as an unreasonable restriction, especially if the Receiving Party needs to clarify information crucial to evaluating the transaction and the designated advisors are unresponsive. This could run afoul of implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under New York contract law, which requires parties to act in a way that does not frustrate the purpose of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 1-304, though technically part of the UCC, codifies the common law duty of good faith in contract performance. The comments to this section clarify that a lack of good faith consists of acting with bad motive. By removing the ability to communicate through advisors, effectively blocking any reasonable clarification of the transaction, the Receiving Party could argue that the Company is acting in bad faith by hindering their ability to evaluate the potential business combination. This lack of information could be a material impediment which a court might see as a violation of the duty of good faith and fair dealing.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text removes the explicit allowance for communication through the Company's advisors. This change contradicts the principle of good faith, which is legally mandated according to NY UCC § 1-304. The original text provided a mechanism for the Receiving Party to seek clarification, which the changed text eliminates, potentially hindering their ability to fairly evaluate the transaction. The scraped law snippet states: 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By restricting communication, the modification arguably allows the company to act in bad faith, thus contradicting the obligation of good faith in contract performance as described in the law.
